title: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens
authors: Karran A. Phillips, Wilson M. Compton

items.title: Polydrug Use: Recognizing Risks and Consequences
items.content: The concurrent use of multiple drugs with different pharmacologic effects can significantly increase health risks. This practice is common among users of cocaine, methamphetamine, and other psychostimulants, often leading to severe medical complications.
keyPoints: Polydrug use involves the concurrent use of several drugs with varying pharmacologic effects.
Common combinations include cocaine with nicotine or heroin in methadone-treated patients.
The combined use of IV heroin and cocaine is particularly dangerous and a frequent cause of emergency department visits.
Chronic psychostimulant use can exacerbate preexisting conditions like hypertension and cardiac disease.
Polydrug use increases vulnerability to infections, including HIV, especially with needle sharing in IV drug users.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Cocaine, Other Psychostimulants, and Hallucinogens

items.title: Chronic Use of Psychostimulants: Health Implications
items.content: Chronic use of psychostimulants such as cocaine and methamphetamine can lead to significant adverse health effects, including immune system dysfunction and increased risk for infections.
keyPoints: Chronic patterns of psychostimulant use may cause adverse health consequences.
Such use can exacerbate preexisting disorders like hypertension and cardiac disease.
Immune system dysfunction is often associated with chronic psychostimulant use, increasing vulnerability to infections.
There is an increased risk for HIV infection due to behaviors such as needle sharing among IV drug users.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Cocaine, Other Psychostimulants, and Hallucinogens

items.title: Cocaine's Rapid Onset and Addictive Potential
items.content: Smoked or vaporized cocaine reaches the brain quickly, similar to injected forms, due to its extensive pulmonary vasculature. This rapid onset results in a transient but intense high, enhancing its addictive potential.
keyPoints: Cocaine binds to dopamine transporters and blocks reuptake, increasing levels of DA, NE, and 5HT.
Rapid brain access via smoking or vaporizing contributes significantly to addiction.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.title: Epidemiology of Cocaine Use in the United States
items.content: In 2023, an estimated 5 million people aged 12 or older were past-year consumers of cocaine. Among these, about 1.3 million had a cocaine use disorder.
keyPoints: 5 million people (1.8% of the population) used cocaine in the past year.
470,000 individuals initiated cocaine use, including 23,000 adolescents aged 12–17 years.
1.3 million people had a cocaine use disorder.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.title: Cocaine Overdose and Polydrug Involvement Trends
items.content: The rate of drug overdose deaths involving cocaine has significantly increased, with a notable rise in cases involving synthetic opioids like fentanyl.
keyPoints: Age-adjusted rate of cocaine-related overdose deaths quintupled from 1.5 per 100,000 in 2011 to 8.2 in 2022.
Involvement of both cocaine and opioids increased the overdose death rate by 7.4 times from 2011 (0.8) to 2021 (5.9).
Cocaine without opioid co-involvement still saw a 2.1-fold increase in overdose deaths from 2011 (0.7) to 2021 (1.5).
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.title: Understanding Methamphetamine's Potency and CNS Impact
items.content: Methamphetamine is significantly more potent than amphetamine, primarily due to its higher concentration of synaptic dopamine (DA) which leads to greater toxic effects on nerve terminals. This drug exists in two stereoisomers; D-methamphetamine has 3–5 times the central nervous system activity compared to L-methamphetamine.
keyPoints: D-Methamphetamine is a more powerful psychostimulant with 3–5 times the CNS activity of L-methamphetamine.
Methamphetamine stimulates release and partially blocks reuptake of catecholamines in the CNS, leading to higher synaptic dopamine concentrations.
It inhibits monoamine oxidase, attenuating the metabolism of monoamines.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · METHAMPHETAMINE

items.title: Epidemiology and Public Health Impact of Methamphetamine Use in 2023
items.content: In 2023, methamphetamine use was reported by 2.6 million people aged 12 or older in the United States, with a significant portion developing a use disorder. The drug's low cost and pharmacokinetics contribute to its chronic high-dose self-administration pattern.
keyPoints: In 2023, 2.6 million individuals aged 12+ used methamphetamine (0.9% of the population).
78,000 people initiated use in 2023, equating to 213 new users per day.
1.8 million had a methamphetamine use disorder, representing 69% of past-year users.
Psychostimulant overdose deaths increased from 0.7 per 100,000 in 2011 to 10.4 per 100,000 in 2022, totaling 36,251 deaths in 2023.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · METHAMPHETAMINE

items.title: Understanding MDMA's Pharmacology and Risks
items.content: MDMA (3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine), commonly known as Molly or ecstasy, is a synthetic drug with both stimulant and psychedelic effects. It primarily binds to serotonin transporters, increasing the release of serotonin, norepinephrine (NE), and dopamine (DA). This binding can lead to significant neurotoxicity, including potential permanent damage to serotonin-containing nerve endings at moderate to high doses.
keyPoints: MDMA increases serotonin, NE, and DA release by binding to serotonin transporters.
Potential for permanent damage to serotonin-containing nerve endings with moderate to high doses.
Schedule I drug status in the U.S. due to no proven therapeutic value despite FDA breakthrough therapy designation for PTSD.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · MDMA AND CATHINONES

items.title: Adulteration and Usage Patterns of MDMA
items.content: MDMA is frequently adulterated with substances like methamphetamine, ketamine, caffeine, dextromethorphan (DXM), ephedrine, and cocaine. It is rarely used alone and often mixed with alcohol or marijuana, complicating the assessment of its specific effects. Usage statistics from NSDUH 2023 indicate that 0.8% of individuals aged 12 or older have used MDMA in the past year, with approximately 507,000 first-time users.
keyPoints: Common adulterants include methamphetamine, ketamine, caffeine, DXM, ephedrine, and cocaine.
MDMA is often mixed with alcohol or marijuana.
0.8% of individuals aged 12+ used MDMA in the past year; 507,000 first-time users reported.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · MDMA AND CATHINONES

items.title: Demographics and Patterns of Cathinone Use
items.content: Cathinone is a psychostimulant similar to amphetamine found in the khât plant. It increases dopamine release and reduces its reuptake. Originally limited to East Africa and the Middle East, cathinone's availability has expanded globally due to advances in transportation. Approximately 10 million people worldwide chew khât, with up to 80% of adults using it in some indigenous regions.
keyPoints: Cathinone increases dopamine release and reduces reuptake.
Schedule I drug status in the U.S., making possession and use illegal.
Estimated 10 million global users; up to 80% adult usage in certain areas.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · MDMA AND CATHINONES

items.title: Prevalence and Misuse of Prescription Psychostimulants
items.content: Prescription psychostimulants such as methylphenidate and amphetamines are commonly misused, particularly among young adults. The 2023 NSDUH reports significant misuse rates and combinations with other substances.
keyPoints: Past-year prescription stimulant misuse was reported by 3.9 million (1.4%) people aged 12 years or older in 2023.
786,000 individuals misused both prescription stimulants and cocaine; 190,000 misused prescription stimulants and methamphetamine; 182,000 misused all three substances.
Past-year initiates of prescription stimulant misuse totaled 712,000, averaging ~1950 people per day, including >1000 young adults aged 12–25 each day.
In 2023, 0.6% of the population aged 12 years or older had a prescription stimulant use disorder.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PRESCRIBED PSYCHOSTIMULANTS

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Overdose Management of Psychostimulants
items.content: Psychostimulants cause both central nervous system (CNS) and peripheral effects, with overdose presenting as sympathetic overactivity. Treatment is symptomatic and requires intensive care.
keyPoints: Acute CNS effects include euphoria, increased energy, reduced need for sleep, decreased appetite, heightened alertness, and decreased distractibility.
Peripheral effects may involve tremor, diaphoresis, hypertonia, tachypnea, hyperreflexia, and hyperthermia.

Overdose symptoms: psychomotor agitation, hypertension, tachycardia, headache, mydriasis, convulsions, cerebral hemorrhage or infarction, cardiac arrhythmias or ischemia, respiratory failure, rhabdomyolysis.
Treatment for overdose is symptomatic and should occur in an intensive care or telemetry unit.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PRESCRIBED PSYCHOSTIMULANTS

items.title: Adulteration Risks of Psychostimulants
items.content: Psychostimulants, particularly cocaine, are often adulterated with other substances that can lead to severe health consequences.
keyPoints: Common adulterants include fentanyl-related compounds, levamisole (causing agranulocytosis, leukoencephalopathy, cutaneous vasculitis), clenbuterol (resulting in tachycardia, hyperglycemia, palpitations, hypokalemia), and xylazine.

Xylazine can cause dry mouth, drowsiness, hypertension, tachycardia followed by hypotension and bradycardia, hyperglycemia, hypothermia, coma, respiratory depression, dysrhythmia, and necrotic soft tissue lesions.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PRESCRIBED PSYCHOSTIMULANTS

items.title: Understanding the Withdrawal Timeline and Symptoms of Psychostimulants
items.content: Withdrawal from psychostimulants typically involves hypersomnia, increased appetite, and depressed mood. The acute withdrawal phase lasts about 7–10 days, but residual symptoms may persist for several months due to potential neurotoxicity.
keyPoints: Acute withdrawal duration: 7–10 days
Residual symptoms can last several months
Debate on whether symptoms decline monotonically or in phases
Psychostimulant withdrawal is not a major driver of ongoing use; conditioned craving plays a significant role
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · TABLE 468-1 Complications of Psychostimulant Use

items.title: Health Risks and Long-term Effects Associated with Khât Use
items.content: Khât, like other psychostimulants, can cause both short-term effects such as euphoria and insomnia, and long-term consequences including gastrointestinal disorders and cardiovascular risks. Heavy use may lead to psychological dependence and a mild withdrawal syndrome.
keyPoints: Short-term effects: euphoria, increased alertness, loss of appetite, insomnia, headaches, tremors
Long-term effects: constipation, ulcers, stomach inflammation, risk for acute myocardial infarction and stroke
Psychological dependence is rare but possible with heavy use
Withdrawal symptoms from khât include depression, nightmares, low blood pressure, lack of energy
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · TABLE 468-1 Complications of Psychostimulant Use

items.title: Management of Cocaine-Induced Psychomotor Agitation and Hypertension
items.content: In cases of acute cocaine intoxication, initial management should focus on ruling out hypoglycemia and hypoxemia before addressing psychomotor agitation with benzodiazepines. For severe or symptomatic hypertension, phentolamine, nitroglycerin, or nitroprusside are recommended over beta blockers due to the risk of unopposed alpha-adrenergic stimulation.
keyPoints: Benzodiazepines (e.g., diazepam 10 mg IV) should be used for agitation after ruling out hypoglycemia and hypoxemia.
Severe hypertension can be treated with phentolamine, nitroglycerin, or nitroprusside.
Avoid beta blockers in cocaine-induced cardiovascular complications; consider mixed alpha/beta blockers like labetalol if necessary.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · DIAGNOSIS | Acute Intoxication

items.title: Approach to Cocaine-Induced Chest Pain and Hyperthermia
items.content: Evaluation of chest pain in cocaine users should include an electrocardiogram, chest radiograph, and biomarkers to exclude myocardial infarction. For hyperthermic patients, cooling measures should aim for a core body temperature below 39°C (102°F) within 30 minutes.
keyPoints: Chest pain evaluation in cocaine users includes ECG, chest radiograph, and biomarkers.
Cooling of hyperthermic patients should achieve <39°C (102°F) within ≤30 min.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · DIAGNOSIS | Acute Intoxication

items.title: Behavioral Therapies as Mainstay Treatment for Psychostimulant Use Disorder
items.content: Behavioral therapies such as cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), community reinforcement approach (CRA), contingency management (CM), and motivational enhancement therapy (MET) are central to treating psychostimulant use disorders. These therapies focus on modifying patient thinking, behaviors, and coping skills to support long-term recovery.
keyPoints: Behavioral therapies show modest benefit in treating SUDs.
Contingency management is highly effective when implemented with fidelity to operant conditioning principles.
Benefits of contingency management can last up to 2 years post-treatment.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Psychostimulant Use Disorders

items.title: Current Research and Challenges in Medication for Psychostimulant Addiction
items.content: There are no FDA-approved medications specifically for psychostimulant addiction. Current research explores various neurotransmitter-based strategies, but results have been inconclusive or equivocal. Studies face challenges such as small sample sizes, sample bias, low retention, and adherence rates.
keyPoints: No FDA-approved medications exist for psychostimulant addiction.
Research includes DA, serotonin, GABA, glutamate-targeting strategies.
Studies with dexamphetamine, methylphenidate, antidepressants, antipsychotics, and others have been inconclusive or equivocal.
Challenges include small sample sizes, bias, low retention, and adherence rates.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Psychostimulant Use Disorders

items.title: Hallucinogens and Their Neurotransmitter Effects
items.content: Hallucinogens disrupt neurotransmitters serotonin and glutamate, affecting mood, sensory perception, sleep, appetite, body temperature, sexual behavior, muscle control, pain perception, responses to the environment, emotion, learning, and memory.
keyPoints: Disrupts serotonin and glutamate systems
Effects on mood, sensory perception, sleep, appetite, body temperature, sexual behavior, muscle control
Perturbations in pain perception, environmental response, emotion, learning, and memory
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Hallucinogen Use
items.content: Hallucinogens cause false sensory experiences (hallucinations), intensified feelings, heightened sensory experiences, time perturbations, nausea, increased heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, body temperature, loss of appetite, xerostomia, sleep problems, synesthesia, impaired coordination, and hyperhidrosis.
keyPoints: False sensory experiences (hallucinations)
Intensified feelings, heightened sensory experiences
Time perturbations, nausea, increased heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, body temperature
Loss of appetite, xerostomia, sleep problems, synesthesia, impaired coordination, hyperhidrosis
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.title: FDA Designations and Research on Hallucinogens for Treatment
items.content: The FDA has issued breakthrough therapy designation for MDMA to treat PTSD and psilocybin formulations for depression. Psilocybin is being studied for alcohol and tobacco use disorders, ketamine for cocaine and methamphetamine use disorders, and both psilocybin and LSD may relieve chronic pain.
keyPoints: FDA breakthrough therapy designation for MDMA (PTSD)
Psilocybin formulations for depression
Psilocybin for alcohol/tobacco use disorders
Ketamine for cocaine/methamphetamine use disorders
Potential relief of chronic pain with psilocybin and LSD
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.title: Understanding the Risks and Effects of Synthetic Cathinones (Bath Salts)
items.content: Synthetic cathinones, commonly known as bath salts, are human-made drugs similar to cathinone found in khat. They often appear as a white or brown powder and can be mislabeled as non-consumable products like 'plant food.' The uncertainty of their composition poses significant risks, as users may unknowingly ingest varying substances with unpredictable effects.
keyPoints: Synthetic cathinones are chemically similar to natural cathinone found in khat but often stronger and more dangerous.
Common forms include powders labeled misleadingly as 'plant food' or other non-consumable products.
Popular nickname Molly is often misused, leading users to receive synthetic cathinones instead of MDMA.
Three common types: mephedrone, methylone, MDPV (3,4-methylenedioxypyrovalerone).
MDPV affects the brain similarly to cocaine but is at least 10 times more potent.
Onset of action from 15–45 min with a duration of 2–7 hours.
High doses or chronic use can lead to psychosis, violent behaviors, tachycardia, hyperthermia, and death.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · EMERGING DRUGS

items.title: Challenges in Regulating Emerging Psychoactive Substances
items.content: The rapid synthesis of new psychoactive substances (NPS) poses a significant challenge to regulation efforts. These drugs, including synthetic cathinones and opioids like nitazines, can be more potent than traditional substances and are often not detected by standard drug tests.
keyPoints: Emerging drugs include synthetic cathinones, cannabinoids, opioids, and other psychoactive substances.
NPS can mimic the effects of illegal drugs like methamphetamine and cocaine but with unpredictable potency.
Substances such as nitazines may be more potent than fentanyl.
These drugs are often not included in standard emergency department drug tests or post-mortem analyses.
The ability to rapidly synthesize these substances by altering molecular structures complicates regulatory efforts.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · EMERGING DRUGS

items.subtopic: SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Overlap of Mental Illness and Substance Use Disorders (SUDs)
items.content: A significant overlap exists between mental illness and SUDs, with a notable treatment gap. In 2023, among adults aged 18 or older, 20.4 million had both any mental illness and an SUD, yet only 62.4% received treatment for either condition.
keyPoints: 21% of adults without mental illness consumed illicit drugs in 2023.
51.9% with severe mental illness and 42.4% with any mental illness consumed illicit drugs.
20.4 million (7.2% of the population) had both any mental illness and an SUD in 2023.
62.4% received treatment for either substance use or mental health.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Global Stimulant Use and Associated Risks
items.content: Stimulants are the third most used drugs globally, with significant health risks. In 2021, an estimated 22 million people used cocaine and 36 million used amphetamines. These substances are linked to elevated mortality, HIV/hepatitis C infection, poor mental health outcomes, and cardiovascular events.
keyPoints: 22 million people used cocaine globally in 2021.
36 million people used amphetamines globally in 2021.
Global deaths attributed to cocaine use were 26,082 in 2019.
Global deaths attributed to amphetamine use were 46,661 in 2019.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Treatment Gap and Stigma in Stimulant Use Disorder (SUD)
items.content: There is a significant treatment gap for stimulant use disorders globally, with only one in seven individuals receiving treatment. Stigma and marginalization further complicate access to care, affecting sustainable development and equity.
keyPoints: 7.4 million individuals have a stimulant use disorder worldwide.
Only one in seven people with SUDs receives treatment.
Cocaine treatment demand rose by almost 60% from 2011 to 2022 in Europe.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Lack of FDA-approved Medications for Psychostimulant and Hallucinogen Use Disorders
items.content: Currently, there are no FDA-approved medications specifically for treating psychostimulant or hallucinogen use disorders. Behavioral therapies like contingency management and cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) have shown effectiveness in psychostimulant use disorders, but their utility in hallucinogen use disorders remains under-researched.
keyPoints: No FDA-approved medications exist for psychostimulant or hallucinogen use disorders
Behavioral therapies such as contingency management and CBT are effective for psychostimulant use disorders
Further research is needed to determine the efficacy of these behavioral therapies in treating hallucinogen use disorders
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.title: Emerging Technologies in Treatment of Psychostimulant Use Disorders
items.content: Research into medications for psychostimulant use disorders is ongoing. Emerging technologies, including neurostimulation/neuromodulation (TMS, TBS, tDCS), wearable biosensors, and mobile technology like ecologic and geographic momentary assessment (EMA/GMA) and real-time interventions via smartphones, show potential in future treatments.
keyPoints: Ongoing research into medications for psychostimulant use disorders
Emerging technologies with potential include neurostimulation/neuromodulation (TMS, TBS, tDCS)
Wearable biosensors and mobile technology such as EMA/GMA and real-time interventions via smartphones are promising
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.question: Which combination of drug use is particularly dangerous and often leads to emergency department visits?
options: Cocaine and heroin in methadone-treated patients
items.explanation: The combined use of intravenous (IV) heroin and cocaine is especially dangerous and accounts for many hospital emergency department visits.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Cocaine, Other Psychostimulants, and Hallucinogens

items.question: What health risk is associated with chronic use of psychostimulants like cocaine?
options: Increased vulnerability to infections including HIV
items.explanation: Chronic use of psychostimulants is often associated with immune system dysfunction and increased vulnerability to infections, including risk for HIV infection.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Cocaine, Other Psychostimulants, and Hallucinogens

items.question: Which group represents the largest single group of individuals with HIV infection in several major metropolitan areas?
items.explanation: People who use intravenous (IV) drugs represent the largest single group of individuals with HIV infection in several major metropolitan areas.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Cocaine, Other Psychostimulants, and Hallucinogens

items.question: Which form of cocaine has a melting point that allows it to be vaporized and inhaled?
items.explanation: Cocaine freebase has a melting point of 98°C, allowing it to be vaporized and inhaled.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.question: What is the bioavailability of intranasally administered cocaine?
items.explanation: The bioavailability of cocaine when used intranasally is about 60%.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.question: Which neurotransmitter's reuptake is blocked by cocaine, increasing its synaptic levels?
items.explanation: Cocaine binds to the dopamine transporter and blocks DA reuptake.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.question: What is a common method of adulterating cocaine to increase profits?
options: Diluting with nonpsychoactive substances like cornstarch
items.explanation: Street dealers often dilute cocaine with nonpsychoactive substances such as cornstarch to increase profits.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.question: Which form of methamphetamine has a higher central nervous system activity?
items.explanation: D-Methamphetamine is a more powerful psychostimulant with 3–5 times the CNS activity compared to L-methamphetamine.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · METHAMPHETAMINE

items.question: What is the bioavailability of methamphetamine when smoked?
items.explanation: When smoked, methamphetamine exhibits a bioavailability of 90.3%.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · METHAMPHETAMINE

items.question: What is one pharmacological action of methamphetamine in the CNS?
options: It blocks the release of catecholamines.
It stimulates the release and partially blocks reuptake of catecholamines.
It enhances metabolism of monoamines by activating monoamine oxidase.
It has no effect on neurotransmitter systems.
items.explanation: Methamphetamine stimulates the release and partially blocks the reuptake of newly synthesized catecholamines in the CNS.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · METHAMPHETAMINE

items.question: Which of the following substances are commonly found in so-called bath salts?
items.explanation: Bath salts often contain synthetic substances like methylone or ethylone.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · MDMA AND CATHINONES

items.question: What is the average time for MDMA to produce its first effects when taken orally?
items.explanation: MDMA typically produces its first effects at around 45 minutes on average.

items.question: Which neurotransmitter does MDMA primarily increase the release of?
items.explanation: MDMA binds to serotonin transporters and increases the release of serotonin.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · MDMA AND CATHINONES

items.question: What is the legal status of MDMA in the United States?
items.explanation: MDMA is classified as a Schedule I drug, indicating no proven therapeutic value.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · MDMA AND CATHINONES

items.question: Which psychostimulant was removed from the market due to its association with hemorrhagic stroke in women?
items.explanation: Phenylpropanolamine was removed from the market in 2005 due to its association with hemorrhagic stroke in women.

items.question: What percentage of people aged 12 years or older had a prescription stimulant use disorder in the past year, according to 2023 data?
items.explanation: In 2023, 0.6% of people aged 12 years or older had a prescription stimulant use disorder in the past year.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PRESCRIBED PSYCHOSTIMULANTS

items.question: What is a common cardiovascular effect of low doses of psychostimulants?
options: Increased heart rate and blood pressure
items.explanation: Low doses of psychostimulants result in increased vagal tone and decreased heart rate.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PRESCRIBED PSYCHOSTIMULANTS

items.question: Which substance, when used with cocaine, results in the metabolite cocaethylene?
items.explanation: The use of alcohol with cocaine results in the formation of the metabolite cocaethylene.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PRESCRIBED PSYCHOSTIMULANTS

items.question: Which of the following is a typical symptom during acute withdrawal from psychostimulants?
items.explanation: Hypersomnia is a common symptom of acute withdrawal from psychostimulants.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · TABLE 468-1 Complications of Psychostimulant Use

items.question: What is the primary driver of ongoing use according to current theories of psychostimulant addiction?
items.explanation: Current theories emphasize conditioned craving as the primary driver of ongoing psychostimulant use.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · TABLE 468-1 Complications of Psychostimulant Use

items.question: What is a potential cardiovascular complication from long-term khât use?
items.explanation: Long-term khât use can increase the risk for acute myocardial infarction due to its cardiovascular effects.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · TABLE 468-1 Complications of Psychostimulant Use

items.question: Which medication is contraindicated in rapid-sequence intubation of a patient with cocaine use?
items.explanation: Succinylcholine is relatively contraindicated in rapid-sequence intubation for patients with cocaine use.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · DIAGNOSIS | Acute Intoxication

items.question: What should be ruled out first if a patient using cocaine presents with psychomotor agitation?
items.explanation: Hypoglycemia and hypoxemia should be ruled out first in cases of psychomotor agitation.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · DIAGNOSIS | Acute Intoxication

items.question: Which medication is recommended to treat severe or symptomatic hypertension in a patient with cocaine use?
items.explanation: Severe or symptomatic hypertension can be treated with phentolamine, nitroglycerin, or nitroprusside.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · DIAGNOSIS | Acute Intoxication

items.question: Which behavioral therapy is described as providing structured and specific incentives to patients who remain substance-free?
items.explanation: Contingency management is described as providing structured and specific incentives to patients who remain substance-free.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Psychostimulant Use Disorders

items.question: What neurotransmitter-based strategies are currently being researched for psychostimulant addiction?
items.explanation: Current research includes strategies targeting DA, serotonin, GABA, and glutamate.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Psychostimulant Use Disorders

items.question: Which medication is being studied as a partial agonist of the α4β2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor?
items.explanation: Varenicline is being studied as a partial agonist of the α4β2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Psychostimulant Use Disorders

items.question: Which neurotransmitter system do hallucinogens specifically disrupt?
items.explanation: Hallucinogens specifically disrupt the neurotransmitters serotonin and glutamate.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation of hallucinogen use?
items.explanation: Clinical manifestations include false sensory experiences, known as hallucinations.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.question: What is the FDA's designation for MDMA in relation to PTSD treatment?
items.explanation: The FDA issued breakthrough therapy designation for MDMA to expedite research into treatment of posttraumatic stress disorder.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.question: Which hallucinogen is known for its short duration of effects, typically lasting about 30 minutes?
items.explanation: Salvia's effects generally last about 30 minutes.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.question: Which synthetic cathinone is noted for being at least 10 times more potent than cocaine and commonly found in the blood of patients admitted to emergency departments after using 'bath salts'?
items.explanation: MDPV is noted for being at least 10 times more potent than cocaine and commonly found in the blood of patients admitted to emergency departments after using 'bath salts'.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · EMERGING DRUGS

items.question: What is a common reason why synthetic cathinones are considered dangerous?
options: Their components might change from batch to batch.
They are always tested for in emergency departments.
They are less potent than natural products.
items.explanation: Synthetic cathinones are considered dangerous because their components might change from batch to batch, making them unpredictable and potentially more harmful.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · EMERGING DRUGS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common synthetic cathinone?
items.explanation: Bromazepam is a synthetic benzodiazepine, not a synthetic cathinone.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · EMERGING DRUGS

items.subtopic: SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: According to the passage, which age group had the highest percentage of adults with both any mental illness and an SUD in 2023?
items.explanation: The passage states that the percentage was highest among young adults aged 18 to 25.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: In 2023, which racial or ethnic group had the highest percentage of adults with both any mental illness and an SUD?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that multiracial adults had the highest percentage at 13.3%.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What was the global cocaine production in 2022 compared to the previous year?
options: The same as the previous year
items.explanation: The passage notes that global cocaine production reached a record high with 20% more produced in 2022 compared to the previous year.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What is the estimated number of people who used amphetamines worldwide in 2021?
items.explanation: The passage states that an estimated 36 million people used amphetamines worldwide in 2021.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which of the following therapies have been shown effective for psychostimulant use disorders?
items.explanation: Behavioral therapies such as contingency management and cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) have been shown effective for psychostimulant use disorders.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.question: What is the current status of FDA-approved treatment medications for psychostimulant and hallucinogen use disorders?
options: There are several approved medications available.
Only treatments for hallucinogen use disorders have been approved.
FDA approval is pending for a few medications.
items.explanation: Despite their prevalence and public health impact, there are no FDA-approved treatment medications for psychostimulant and hallucinogen use disorders.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.question: Which emerging technology is mentioned as having potential in the future treatment of psychostimulant use disorders?
options: None, only behavioral therapies are considered
items.explanation: Emerging technologies such as neurostimulation/neuromodulation (TMS, TBS, tDCS) have considerable potential for future treatment of psychostimulant use disorders.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.statement: Polydrug use is uncommon among users of cocaine and other psychostimulants.
items.explanation: Polydrug use, involving the concurrent use of several drugs with different pharmacologic effects, is common.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Cocaine, Other Psychostimulants, and Hallucinogens

items.statement: The concurrent use of cocaine and opiates is frequently associated with needle sharing.
items.explanation: The concurrent use of cocaine and opiates ('speedball') is frequently associated with needle sharing by people using drugs intravenously (IV).
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Cocaine, Other Psychostimulants, and Hallucinogens

items.statement: Cocaine is a DEA Schedule I drug.
items.explanation: Cocaine is classified as a DEA Schedule II drug.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.statement: The rate of drug overdose deaths involving both cocaine and opioids in 2021 was higher than the rate in 2011.
items.explanation: In 2021, the rate was 5.9 per 100,000 compared to 0.8 in 2011.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.statement: In 2023, methamphetamine-related overdose deaths decreased compared to previous years.
items.explanation: Stimulant-involved overdose deaths have risen markedly in recent years.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · METHAMPHETAMINE

items.statement: D-Methamphetamine is less potent than L-methamphetamine.
items.explanation: D-Methamphetamine has 3–5 times the CNS activity compared with L-methamphetamine.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · METHAMPHETAMINE

items.statement: Cathinone is legal for use in the United States.
items.explanation: Cathinone is a Schedule I drug, making its possession and use illegal in the U.S.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · MDMA AND CATHINONES

items.statement: MDMA has been given a breakthrough therapy designation by the FDA for PTSD.
items.explanation: MDMA has received a breakthrough therapy designation for potential use in treating PTSD.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · MDMA AND CATHINONES

items.statement: MDMA can occasionally result in liver, kidney, or heart failure.
items.explanation: MDMA has been shown to raise body temperature and can occasionally lead to liver, kidney, or heart failure.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PRESCRIBED PSYCHOSTIMULANTS

items.statement: Levamisole is a common adulterant in cocaine that does not cause any health issues.
items.explanation: Levamisole, found as an adulterant in cocaine, can cause agranulocytosis, leukoencephalopathy, and cutaneous vasculitis.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PRESCRIBED PSYCHOSTIMULANTS

items.statement: Psychostimulant withdrawal symptoms are thought to be a major driver of ongoing use.
items.explanation: Most current theories suggest that conditioned craving, not withdrawal symptoms, is the primary driver of ongoing psychostimulant use.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · TABLE 468-1 Complications of Psychostimulant Use

items.statement: Injection of psychostimulants can increase the risk of contracting infectious diseases.
items.explanation: Injection of psychostimulants increases the risk of HIV, hepatitis B or C, skin abscesses, and endocarditis.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · TABLE 468-1 Complications of Psychostimulant Use

items.statement: Beta blockers are recommended as first-line treatment for cocaine-induced cardiovascular complications.
items.explanation: Beta blockers should be avoided due to potential unopposed alpha-adrenergic stimulation.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · DIAGNOSIS | Acute Intoxication

items.statement: Benzodiazepines can usually address cardiovascular side effects in cocaine intoxication.
items.explanation: Benzodiazepines are typically sufficient to manage cardiovascular side effects.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · DIAGNOSIS | Acute Intoxication

items.statement: There are FDA-approved medications for psychostimulant addiction.
items.explanation: The passage states that there are no FDA-approved medications for psychostimulant addiction.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Psychostimulant Use Disorders

items.statement: Contingency management has shown benefits lasting up to 2 years beyond treatment discontinuation.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that the benefit of contingency management continues for up to 2 years beyond treatment discontinuation.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · Psychostimulant Use Disorders

items.statement: Hallucinogen use disorder in DSM-5 includes a withdrawal criterion.
items.explanation: The withdrawal criterion does not apply to hallucinogens, as their use patterns are generally not chronic.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.statement: Chronic effects of hallucinogen use can include persistent psychosis and memory loss.
items.explanation: Chronic effects of hallucinogen use can indeed include persistent psychosis, memory loss, anxiety, depression, and flashbacks.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.statement: Nitazines are emerging drugs that can be more powerful than fentanyl.
items.explanation: The passage states nitazines are synthetic opioids that can be more powerful than fentanyl.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · EMERGING DRUGS

items.statement: Emergency department drug tests routinely include testing for all emerging substances.
items.explanation: Emerging substances are often not included in emergency department drug tests, making it difficult to determine the cause of death after a fatal overdose.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · EMERGING DRUGS

items.subtopic: SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: In 2023, more than half of adults with severe mental illness consumed illicit drugs.
items.explanation: The passage states that 51.9% of adults with severe mental illness consumed illicit drugs.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: In 2023, the global deaths attributed to amphetamine use were higher than those attributed to cocaine.
items.explanation: The passage provides figures of 26,082 for cocaine and 46,661 for amphetamines.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: Research on medications to treat psychostimulant use disorders has been completed.
items.explanation: Research on medications for treating psychostimulant use disorders is ongoing.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.statement: Behavioral therapies are currently the only effective treatment for hallucinogen use disorders.
items.explanation: While behavioral therapies have been shown effective in psychostimulant use disorders, further research is needed regarding their utility for hallucinogen use disorders.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.assertion: Cocaine's rapid onset of effects contributes to its addictive potential.
items.reason: Smoked or vaporized cocaine reaches the brain very quickly, similar in speed of onset to injected cocaine.
items.explanation: The rapid and intense high from quick brain access enhances addiction.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.assertion: Cocaine use leads to long-term changes in the brain.
items.reason: Animal studies have shown adaptations in neurons that release glutamate after cocaine exposure.
items.explanation: While both statements are true, the reason is not directly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PSYCHOSTIMULANTS | COCAINE

items.assertion: MDMA is often mixed with other substances.
items.reason: This makes the specific impacts of its use difficult to ascertain.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why MDMA's effects can be hard to determine.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · MDMA AND CATHINONES

items.assertion: Cathinone is a naturally occurring substance found in the khât plant.
items.reason: It structurally resembles amphetamine and increases dopamine release.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains cathinone's properties.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · MDMA AND CATHINONES

items.assertion: Psychostimulant use can lead to restlessness and insomnia.
items.reason: Higher doses of psychostimulants cause increased sympathetic nervous system activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PRESCRIBED PSYCHOSTIMULANTS

items.assertion: Cocaethylene has no impact on cardiovascular health.
items.reason: Cocaethylene's effects on the cardiovascular system are additive to that of cocaine's effects.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; cocaethylene does have an additive effect on cardiovascular health.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · PRESCRIBED PSYCHOSTIMULANTS

items.assertion: Psilocybin is being studied for its potential to treat certain substance use disorders.
items.reason: Psilocybin has shown promise in treating depression and alcohol and tobacco use disorders.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why psilocybin is being studied for SUDs.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.assertion: Ketamine is used as an FDA-approved nasal spray for treatment-resistant depression.
items.reason: Ketamine's dissociative properties make it effective in altering mood and perception.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not directly explain why ketamine is used as a nasal spray.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · HALLUCINOGENS

items.assertion: Stigma and marginalization contribute to the difficulty in treating drug use disorders.
items.reason: The passage mentions that stigma and marginalization hinder sustainable inclusive development and treatment of drug use disorders.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cocaine Other Psychostimulants and Hallucinogens · SUBSTANCE USE AND MENTAL HEALTH | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: The number of people receiving treatment for stimulant use disorder is high.
items.reason: The World Health Organization estimates that only one in seven people with SUDs receives treatment.
